context.scenario:
**A little general background:** I'm a first-year law student. In my country, after high school, you typically attend law school for a five-year degree, take the bar exams, and then begin practising law. My college has given us an internship break until February, so I'm currently interning for two months.

I (F17) have been interning with a firm since December. A new intern (F20-22) joined in January. For context, we've had minor arguments since her first day, when she made a comment about my financial background, implying I don't need to work because I "must have daddy's money lying around," since my particular law school is quite expensive.

Over the past few days, she has been taking off her shoes while working. Since we share the same table, the smell becomes unbearable. (She wears shoes without socks, and with the building's elevator out of order, we’ve been climbing five flights of stairs daily in 31°C/87.8°F weather.)

I’ve been addressing this issue privately for a few days now—starting with subtle hints and eventually directly asking her to keep her shoes on. Today, the same issue arose, but this time, I confronted her in front of other coworkers (who happened to be her juniors from the same college). The conversation unfolded as follows:

**Me:** Hey \[name\], I hope this doesn’t come across the wrong way, but I’ve noticed you sometimes take off your shoes in the office. I understand wanting to be comfortable, but since this is a shared space, it might be better to keep them on to maintain a professional atmosphere. I hope you don’t mind me bringing this up again.

**Her:** Why does it matter to you? You already said the same thing the other day.

**Me:** It’s just a bit uncomfortable to see your feet while working (we have glass tables).

**Her:** Then don’t look.

**Me:** You’ve also been kicking me under the table, which is really uncomfortable. Could you please stop?

**Her:** No, I’m wearing heels, and they’re uncomfortable to wear all day. I’ll continue to take them off.

**Me:** I get that heels can be uncomfortable, and I understand wanting to be at ease. Maybe you could try wearing sports shoes or office shoes like the rest of us? It must also be tough to climb the stairs in heels. But could you please keep your shoes on? The smell can get a bit overwhelming.

**Her:** No. I would know if my feet smelled.

**Another coworker (to me):** Show her some respect. She’s three years older than you, *bitch.*

  
So Reddit, AITA for asking my coworker to wear her shoes because I don't want to see her feet?
context.comment:
Stop talking to her, talk to your manager insead .

  
NTA for finding it unplesant, you re the AH for seeking to dictate on the basis of what \[you think\] looks professional, but not unreasonable to want not to have to deal with the smell. 

  
But ultimately - you've made clear  that you want her to keep her shoes or oro something about the emell, she's made clear she is not going to and doesn't accept that they do smell.

You can either escalate the tissue to someone who has the power to tell her she has to keep her shoes om , or you need to put up with it (in which case maybe having an air freshener under or on your desk, or a fan pointing away from you, so that you are less bothered by the smell, or even putting vicks vapoir rub or something similarly strong secnted under your nose to mask the smell are all possibilities.

Also think about the dynamics in the office. Is aising this going to make youlook fussy or awkward, and how might that affect your carerr? So you need feedback or refnerecens from this placement? Might you be looking for work with them later? Alll those things are relant to considering whether you want to escalate and be the intern complaining about others. 

If you do decide to escalate, don't say anything about how it looks - it's not up to you to decide whether havig your shows off is or is not professional - focus on the practicalities and how they accffect you ( X routinely takes their shoes off in the ofice and unfotunbately, thir feet smell and tht is very noticeble when they take their shoes off. I have mentioned it to them to ask that they keep their shoes on,and have mentioend the unfotunate smell, but they haven't done anything to address the issue" -

context.scenario: So for context my dad passed away in August of 2022 on his birthday and I was hella depressed and I talked about him all the time and my friend told me 2 months after he passed if I'm still morning him I'm being selfish because I haven't moved on. I was calling my friend out for that last night because the topic of something came up and I said then again our other friend had a problem with me not moving on either and he said I need to move on cuz I talked about him non-stop for months after he died. To this day I still refuse to talk about my dad and I never truly mourned him because I had to start college 2 weeks after his death and I feel guilty for mentioning him because it pisses these two off but other people say it's normal to talk about a loved one when they pass so am I a bad person that I still talk about my dad or should I just get over it and just be quiet because he's gone.
context.comment:
I'm very sorry for your loss.  Grieving takes time - it's unreaslistic to expect anyone to get over the loss of a parent in 2 months.

Over time, it can be comfroting to be able to remember them and talk about them. I hope yu don't let your friend's reactions and unkind comments put you off feeling you can talk bout yur dad, and remabering positive thinhgs and him and your times together 

  
It soundsas thoug hyour friends at the time werestill very young and aimature, perhaps had no experience of anything similar and didn't kno how to react. Grief \*is\* selfish, In the beginning, it is often so overwhelming that you can't think of anything else. It's selfish in the same way that a drowning person is selfish, unable to see or think of anything else, grabbing onto anything that might help.  

Over time, it gets easier to cope with,. It's overwehelmening for less of the time, you beging to be able think about things which are not your loss and pain , to have the bandwidth to start to think about other people and htheir needs and feelings as well as yours. 

I think what you need to take from this is that that particualr friend is not someoneyou can talk about your dad, or your grief, with. Your other friend may or may not be, depending on whether what you were told was an accurate report of what that other person said.

If you can access it, you mkight find it useful to look into grief counselling - are you still a student? Does your college / universiaty have any student support services who might be able to put you in touch with  someone and you may find that useful to help you to  work through some of your feelings.

It's understandable that your firends ere unsure bout how to react and what to expect, if they've never been in a simialr situation, but even taking that into account their beahviour was pretty insensitve and unkind. 

NTA

context.scenario:
I (20F) have been friends with this guy Brady (20 M) my entire life. Our families have known each other for decades. Throughout our time at school, we would casually hang out and as the years went on I hung out with him and his circle more and more. Over this time, there have been several small things that have pissed me off—and our other friends—but I never put much value in it. Over the last two years, these occasions have been more and more frequent. 

Recently, Brady has started to be blatantly rude to me and our mutual female friend. Any time a girl will make a joke he will shut us down, but if our male friend says the same thing he will laugh his ass off. He has also made it VERY clear he wants to fuck two of our male friends—so he will treat them 100 times better than any girl in our circle. Honestly, he seems to look down on women and minorities; he says he’s allowed to make crude jokes about these groups because he’s gay. 

A few months ago my mother was diagnosed with stage four cancer. For five months, I took over everything in my house while my parents were in the hospital. Brady constantly harassed me about when I could hang out. When I said I had too much going on he simply said “well figure it out and tell us a day”. He never asked how I was doing or how my mother was. Right before Christmas, my mother ended up passing away. When I texted this group of friends saying she passed all he texted me was “oh yeah I figured”. The next time I hung out with this group, he complained about how crappy his mom is the whole time. His mother simply asked what he was doing and has never been abusive or rude to him. He doesn’t understand how much my mother dying has affected me and he hasn’t taken the time to simply ask how I am. He completely ignored what has happened and if I mention it he acts annoyed. 

It has gotten very hard to be around him and not be rude. Recently I have snapped at him multiple times due to small weird comments he has made (jokes about women, telling people to k*** themselves, sexualizing our straight male friends that are extremely uncomfy with it, etc) and he seems to think everyone is bullying him for no reason. Btw, my uncle k***ed himself when Brady and I were 15. I found his dead body and I am still very sensitive about jokes regarding that sensitive subject because of it. Brady knows this, yet anytime he gets mad he will yell in my face telling me to k*** myself.

AITA for snapping on him? Should I continue my friendship or let it die with my mom?
context.comment:
This guy is racist, misogynisitic, bully  who sexually harasses people, and he is also mind-blowingly insensitive. 

He has shown absolutely no insight into his beahiours and no interest in becoming a civilised human beaing.

He is not your friend. He's an AH who you happen to know. 

Focus on the friends you have who acually cre abotu you, and who you can enjoy spending time wittth. 

Move on and don't look back.

  
You are NTA. The fact that you have known someone a long time is not agood reason to keep them in your life . Having known someone a long time can mean it is reasonable to give them a second chance if they mes up once, it's not a good reason to keep someone whi is or has become toxic in your life.

  
Look at it this way - you've been in Brady's life since you were kid, but he didn't care enough oabout you, or the frienhip, to ask how yo were doing when your mum was sick, or to realise that having found someone you cared about who took their own life would mean that you were sensitive about 'jokes' on that subject, or to consider you might need support from your friends when you lost your mom. He doesn't see you as a friend or as someone who is imortnat to him or a part of his life. 

I hope that you are able to maintain friendships with the mutuals you had - make arrangmessnts with them, let them know you are not inviting Brady becaue of his beahiour. You may well find that they are as relived as you to  be able to hang out without being subjected to his boorish beahviours.

  
(And for the record, no, being part of one minority does not make it in any way acceptable to discriminate against or demean or denigrate members of a different group. I am a woman. That is not a free pass that lets me make homophobic or racist comments or crude 'jokes' about people of colour or members of the LBGQT+community)

context.scenario:
I (28F) have been in this apartment for a year now with my partner (31F). My partner's brother also lives here and is friends with the landlord, which is how we got the apartment. We LOVE the unit! It was renovated just before we moved in - the building is otherwise quite old. We also love that the building has mostly older residents, making it VERY quiet. We live next to a couple (M/F - late 20's) who mentioned they've been here for at least 8 years. We thought we found a gem, but it seems many people here want to control how others live their lives...

We moved from the Ukraine to Canada, but didn't think people here were so stuck up. 

We smoke cigarettes and thought Canadians didn't care about that. I was wrong. Rain or shine, we would go out to smoke under the building's cover out front, avoiding the main road. Apparently, many complained, and we were told we are not allowed to smoke that close to the entrance. They all acted like we were harming them. Some suggest smoking at the backway near the parking lot, but there are creepy men who hang around there, and I won't risk harassment. So now we smoke in our unit, keeping to ourselves to avoid bothering anyone. 

However, the neighbors are complaining now, saying they can smell it in their unit. The woman, in particular, has been pretending to cough loudly. Instead of addressing us, they went directly to the landlord to try to get us in trouble saying someone was smoking. We don’t think our habits are anyone's business, so we are denying it. No one should tell me how to live in the place I pay rent for. We spray the hallways so the smell shouldn't be noticeable - and I really doubt it's as bad in their unit as they claim. I read my rights and unless there is proof no one can enter my unit.

  
We are going to file a harassment letter against them, but my partner's brother said that might not be a good idea and that we could be in the wrong.  
  
AITA here?
context.comment:
YTA. Cigarette smoke is harmful, even second hand, and it is incredibly pervasive so it is very likely that they can, in fact, smell it when you smoke in your unit.  So they acted like you were harming them becasue you were (and are) in fact harming them. 

Cigarette smoke absolutely sticks and you are fooling yourself if you think spraying solves that. 

Smokers often  don;t realise how much they and their clothing and surroundings stink becasue you become 'nose blind' and also becasue smoking  reduces your capacity to smell and taste.

Buildings are not air tigjht and an older building, especially if it was converted into units not built that way, may well have cnnected sytems meanting that smoke and smells will pass from one unit to another. 

Your neighbours are not trying to "control how you live your lives", they are trying to enjoy peaceful occupation of their own homes, and being harmed by your inconiderate and antisocial beahviour. 

Them coughing because they are being exposed to, and harmed by, your second hand smoke is not harassment. 

Also, different people will hve different levels of sensitivity to cigarette smoke and other harmful substances, other neighbours not being affected enough to cough doesn't mean the coughers are faking it, it simply means that they are more physically sensitive to the smoke and/or that the lay out of the building means it impacts them more thean your other neighbours. 

Given how you come over in this post and the fact that you have ignored what you were told by your landlords, anf you've admitted here that you have been lying about smoking in the apartment. I wouldn't approach an antisocial liar directly either.

If your lease allows smoking inside the building then you need to look into what can be done to prevent smoke and smells getting into other units - maybe get youself an air purifier and ask your landlord if there is anything further that they can do.

  
If it doesn't, then look forward to the eviction notice. Why would alandlord keep antisocial tenants who lie, ignire the lease and reasonable requests, and harass other tennts, especially when the other tenants are, presumably, good, long term tenants. 

But really, The solution here is for you to follow the instructions you've been given by your landlords and to smoke at the rear of the building . Or you can sit and smoke in your car, if you have one.

context.scenario:
I'm a 43F happy in love and agreed to marry a 37M.

My stepmom has been my stepmom since I was 19. We never lived under the same roof. We never really got along, for my dad's sake I always let it slide. But she’s done inappropriate things like tell me “You’re too old to wear a skirt that length” (to my knees) or tell me my life choices were wrong (midlife crisis - I just went to nursing school). My dad isn’t innocent, he would always be there pretending he didn’t notice (he’s not that dumb - he knew she was inappropriate). The final straw was when she angrily tried to tell me I was being a dependent loser like my mother and should move out alone when my fiance and I were going through a rough patch.  The kicker: my mother (whom my stepmom knows I’m very close to) HAD BEEN DEAD FOR LESS THAN A YEAR at this point! (tragic and sudden)

I realized: I’m an adult. It's high time I stand up for myself. Now, I simply do not allow that woman in my life. When I graduated from nursing school, I invited my father but told him she’s not allowed. In return he refused to come. I said ‘okay’ and let it be.

Now, the man (aforementioned 37M) who has been with me through it all, took me on a trip to the U.K. and at a beautiful waterfall in Scotland asked me to be his wife.

I’m looking at all-inclusive venues in Colorado. We want an intimate ceremony, just close family and friends. Around 20 guests. I made a list of questions (for when we talk with the venues) and potential guest list and shared the document with my father (and future SIL). My dad changed the guestlist to add my stepmom and emailed me “You should invite her. It’s the polite thing to do.” 

That language in an email makes it sound (to me) as if my dad thinks I wronged my stepmom. I realize I could be the bigger person, but I feel like her presence at my wedding would be an insult to my dead mother’s memory. And I’m somewhat sensitive and feel deeply, I would be uncomfortable with her there. It’s MY day. I don’t want that. Maybe if it was a big wedding with 200 guests, but small and intimate? I couldn’t avoid her and not feel uncomfortable. (fyi my dad walking me down the aisle won’t matter, we’re bucking a lot of traditions and, please, I’m in my forties)

But am I going too far by standing firm that my stepmom not attend?
context.comment:
In tis insntance, NTA. As a general rule, it would be considered rude to invite only half of a couple to something like a awedding (with exceptions for things like when you invite coworkers as a group, where none of the partners are icludd, or wher the event is single gender)

BUT in this case, the situiation is that you don't want yourfathert's wife cominfg becaue of her previous poor beahviour.

So I think you need to go back to you dad and actually lay it out for him pretty clearly. Maybe something alson the lines of ;

Dad, I am not able to invite \[wife\]. 

I agree that in most circumstances, it would be polite to invite the spouse of a guest, however, in this case , I can't do that  becaue of how \[stepmother\] has behaved in the past  and in particualar, her profoundly cruel and  offensive comments made to me about my mum,  which were made not long after Mum died, and her insultss towards me personally

There's been a long history of \[stepmother\] making very rude, offensive and imappropriate comments to and about me, and being very disrespecful in how she treats me. I've tried to put up with that for your sake, however, the comments about Mum were deeply hurtful and I no longer feel comfortable being around \[stepmother\]. 

She's never even attempted to apolgise to me for her behaviour  or for those insults, and, especially, as we are planning a very small, intitmate wedding, we want to ensure that eveyone who is there, is someone we care about and know supportd and cares about us. Unfornately, stepmother has made very clear that she does not support or care about me. 

It's really importnat to me that I feel comfortable and am able to relax and enjoy our wedding day, that would simply not be possible if \[stepnother\] was present.. Having her there would ruin my wedding day and I am not prepared to have that happen. I do also feel that it would be an insult to mum's memory to have her there, given what she has said anbout me and mum. 

I hope that you will attend - you're my dad, I love you,. I really hipe that for this one dayt, you will be prepared toput me, as your daughter, first.

However, if you chose not to come then I will of course accept that choice.

context.scenario: My husband is terrified of the dentist and has never been as long as we’ve been together (8 yrs). He’s had a lot of issues with them and although he always looks after his teeth, his oral hygiene seems to be getting worse. Recently, one of his teeth has broken and caused bad breath. I’ve told him he needs to get it checked because it’s making me not want to kiss him. He’s said that I’m making something out of nothing and basically being mean to him. I feel bad but I had to say something.
context.comment:
NTA, but rather than telling him he has to go, maybe sit down with him and talk to him about how you can help him.

  
I have a phobia about dentists due to having a crap dentits (and poorly constructed teeth) when I was a child. I'm much better now but still fin it really stressful even though I haven't needed any workl k for yers, now.

When I was in my early 20s I needed a filling, the dentist I was registered with said they couldn't do it becasue I was tembling so much it wouldn't be safe. They referred me to a specialist dentist who dealth with people who had various kinds of special neds, from dental phobias like mine, to kids and adults with diabilitie, and I think older people with dementia.  Perhaps you could aks your dentist if there is anything similar available and how to get a referral?

It may be that your partner would benefit from seeing a dental hyginest first, if he/you are able to talk to them first they could probably do a simple clean to start with, so he gets to sit in the dentists chair but nothing worse than brushin his teeth happens, and go from there. 

Talk to him about what it is that he finds scary.

  
Personally, my particualr horror is a drill, and by exension, anything that vibrates.  My dentist (both the specialist andthe one I see know) would alsways tell me exactly what they were going to fso (e.g. "I'm going to be tapping your teeth" or "I'm going to be using little spurts of cold air" , and tol me I could riase my hand and they would stop at any time, so I felt I had some control. 

  
The speicalist was able to give me gas-and-air before foinf anything at all and for a karger oiece of work I was once given intravenous valium (which worked for getting me through root canal work but I really didn't like the side affects.

  
But my point is that there are specialists and that if he is able to to talk to a dentist about his fears they should be able to work with him

context.scenario:
AITA. Ok here is the situation. My wife and I just had a baby recently. Baby at this point is a few months old and don't sleep through the night. The wife takes care of the baby over night as she don't work and goes to school online. I take over in the morning to give her a chance to get sleep.

I work full time 2nd shift and I also go to school online full time. so I have to be to work at 2pm and work till 10pm. When I get home I generally like to chill alone for a bit and decompress. On top of this I am dealing with some family issues that have had me in a rough place mentally the past few days. Now generally when I have stuff going on I also like being left alone for a bit where I'll generally sit in quiet and just think. 

Now each time I come home from work my wife greets me and starts wanting to hang out, have a full on conversation about multiple topics.  I tell her that I'm glad to see her but I'd like some chill time when I first get home even if it is just a little bit then we can chat or do whatever. Recently the wife and I have had some big fights about me wanting/needing this alone time. It is an issue for her because she says she don't see me alot with the new baby and schedule. Which I get like I said I take care of the baby so she can sleep and I wake her up when I have to leave for work. So during the week we don't see each other alot. I try to make up for it on weekends but we both have class work to do so sometimes we don't have alot of time then ethier.


I have tried to tell her that all I want is to be alone for a little while and decompress. Even if it is only for 15-20 minutes. So idk am I being unreasonable here? Is me wanting that space and chill time too much?
context.comment:
Mild YTA.  For 2 reaons. First, it doesn't appear that ther eis any time built in for your wife to get to chill / decompress, and secondly, while your personl preference is for some alone tme it sounds as though what she needs is some adult company , so the two of you have conflicting needs.

I think you probably need to sit down together to talk about how you cn mke sure both sets of needs are met.

Some things to consider:

\- You getting up slightly ealier so you have have some alone time before the baby wakes up and you need to go into morning parent mode, so that you care then able to be more sociable with your wife when you get home in the eveing

\- re-jig the pattern of the eveing - this might mean that you spend some time with her when you first get home, but agree that you will hav a bit of alone time after you have those conversations, even if it is a case of her going up to bed 10-15 minutes before you do.  

\- can you take some time in the day to chill? Would you be able to find somewhere wuitet you could go and sit for a while during your lunch break, for example? 

\- you say you wake her up when it is time for you to leave for work. How would she feel about you waking her a litte bit earlier so that you and she had time for a brief chat before you leave for the day - even if this is you taking her up a cup of tea/coffeee 15 minutes bfore you leave for the day.

How do you get to and from work? Obviously you can't relx too much if you are driving but maybe think about how you could make that a more calm experience.

If none of the other optipns work perhaps you and she could agree that on alternerate days you will spend 10 minutes sitting in the car to unwind before you come into the house to start the eveing, and on the other days you will come straight in, so you both get what you need at least some of the time ((she may feel less ignored and lonely if you are not coming in and immediately shutting yourself away, but insted spending a little time before you come in.

Is there any chancethat the two of you could afford a sitter once a week so that you could have an hour or so one day where you can spend some time focussing on each other? 

Both of you studying full time on top of your jobs and child care is very intense. IS there any scope for you to cut the number of classs you are dong and slightly extend the time you'll take to finish your degerees? I know it's not ideal but freeing up a little more time might be beneficial foboth of you and the baby

context.scenario:
My family (mom, dad, brother with partner and two kids, sister with partner and two kids, brother w/o partner and kids and me w/o partner and kids) are going on family vacation soon. My parents are paying for flight tickets and housing, for everyone, so we have decided that the rest of us will pay for food the entire trip. 
A while back we had a situation where some of us were paying for dinner for my parents, but when we went to split the bill, we split it threefold (my brother and my sisters partner were not there), as in my brothers partner was not paying. I did have a reaction to this, because I figured that all adults would be paying for themselves, as everyone ate. I obviously don’t mind paying a part for the kids, but just felt it was weird for her not to pay a part.
We talked about it, she did not pay, but I was fine with that considering the amount was not too big, but expressed that in the future they might be considerate that when two have partners(both have their own income) and two don’t, me and my single brother will always be paying a bigger share if the partners don’t pay for themselves.
As we are going on this vacation, we are paying for the food. We are obviously super lucky that our parents are paying for the rest of the trip, but the amount for the food will be considerably bigger than last time this was an issue. It has not yet been specified if we are splitting between the four siblings or if the partners are paying too. As I said I am happy to be paying for the kids and my parents, but as the partners are getting a free trip too, and have they are both two income families, I feel it only makes sense that they pay for themselves. I’m scared to bring it up, as it is an uncomfortable subject, but u really don’t want to be paying more just because I’m single. If we all had partners it would be different, but as I see it, me and my single brother will be paying “double” if we split it by four. Am I the asshole for bringing this up, and expecting the partner to pay their share?
context.comment:
NTA,  but bring it up before you travel.
Insincere you are willing to subsidise your nibbling, suggest a sime 3 way split. You are your single bro pay 1/3, the other siblings each pay 1/3 (and how they split that with theor partners is up to them.


You can bring it up on the basis that you think it would be nice to cover your parents share as they're paying for accommodation, and that your willi g to cover a share of the kids, so it's a simple 6 way split between all the adults.

context.scenario:
I have a best friend, Sarah, who is getting married next month. The problem is, my ex is also invited because we’re in the same friend group. We broke up about a year ago, and while things are civil, I still feel uncomfortable being around him, especially at such a big event.

I told Sarah that I might not be able to come because I’m not sure how I’ll handle seeing my ex there. She was really upset and said that I was being selfish and ruining her special day. She insists that I should just ‘move past it’ and that our friendship means more than my discomfort. I’m really torn because I want to be there for her, but I also don’t want to force myself into a situation where I’ll feel emotionally drained.
context.comment:
Assuming that this was not an abusive relationship and there hasn't been any harassment or similar behaviour from your ex, then I think it would be reasonable for you to make the effort and go.

  
Maybe arrange in advance to got with another friend so you don't have to walk in / sit by yourself, and (if the wedding is formal enough to havea seating plan) ask Sarah to make sure that you and your ex on not both put on the same table at the reception.

  
Obvuiosuly you don't \*have\* to go, but this is your best friend. For my best friend, Id suck it up and go.

Sometimes, being a friend does mean doing things which are difficult or uncomfortable. 

Depending what the plan for the wedding day is, it may also be possible for you to limit how much time you are going to be around your ex - e.g. if the plan is ceremony -> formal receptions / speeches -> first dance -> general dancing and mingling, then you could plan to leave after the first dance so you don't need to mingle and socialise.

Unless there is a lot more about the breakup which means that it is treasonable for you to refuse to attend, then yes, I thnk that YTA for ot being willing to deal with being in a largegroup of people which happens to include your ex, whom you split from a year ago. If you'd split up last week it might be more understandable.

A;lso - why have you left it until a month before the wedding? You broke up a year ago. Have you not been in the same room since then? Or thought to speak to Sarah abtu it earlier?

scenario:
Throwaway because my partner/friends/family are avid redditors. Though this post is so obviously me, they might even know at this point. 

I’m not really sure what led up to the conversation about this. My partner (20M) and I (22F) started by disagreeing about peeing in the shower alone. He said that it’s not normal for people to do it AT ALL. My whole family are shower pee-ers. I’ve been raised to, if needed, pee, and then take a normal shower and it’s acceptable since the soap washes it down. I’m not a UTI girl either, so no crazy bacteria is spread to those who share the shower. I’ve heard that this can be damaging to the pelvic floor and what have you, but other than that, peeing in the shower isn’t damaging to the shower or worthy of jail time. My partner said it’s just weird to pee in the shower overall at this point. I then share how it’s something I’ve grown up used to and something I consider environmental for me because, though I hate to admit, I use more toilet paper than is likely needed and it certainly saves some squares. We obviously disagree about solo peeing in the shower, but here’s where it gets iffy (and slightly weird). We started arguing about how gross it is when we shower TOGETHER. Oftentimes I will do it before he gets in, but sometimes I’m waking up in a morning shower, and I will strategically aim myself straight down the drain (as I can only direct myself in one position) and whizz. I move as FAR away as possible from him, give him plenty of warning, and even ASK if it’s ok, to which he says it’s fine. Yet, he says he HATES this; not only did he say it’s not normal to pee in the shower alone, but peeing in any shower is weird, especially with him present. Now, I’m not usually one to fight against things like this, because I understand it’s a boundary, but holy moly y’all. He then said he’d rather have blood than pee getting on him. Now, that seems a bit more dangerous than my piddle puddle. Ok, ok. At this point, I said I would stop doing it while he’s present, and make it a personal experience, but even that is gross to him, and now he’s going back and forth about who’s “right” here. He even said he’d try it out to see how it feels. I can see why it might be gross for some people, but as long as I’m not aiming at him, am I so wrong? NSFW: keep in mind the bodily fluids that are often shared between partners outside the shower. That’s all I’ll put up to mention as well. Someone please tell me the regularity/normalcy (or not) of peeing in the shower alone OR with a partner. I’ve decided to stop for now but I didn’t realize it was that big of a deal. Help. AITAH???

TLDR: my partner disagrees that it’s normal to pee alone in the shower, and especially when we’re showering together.
question: Based on this person's historical commenting patterns, which moral principle would they MOST likely use to judge this scenario?
answer_options: A. Autonomy/Boundaries: The judgment is primarily justified by violation of personal sovereignty: controlling behavior, unwanted interference, privacy invasion, or boundary crossing (non-material).
B. Property/Consent: The judgment is primarily justified by violations involving ownership, money, physical possessions, or bodily access without permission.
C. Fairness/Reciprocity: The judgment is primarily justified by proportionality, equity, or reciprocal treatment (fair share, equal standards, tit-for-tat).
D. Care/Harm Prevention: The judgment is primarily justified by concern about direct physical or emotional suffering of a person (victim-centered empathy, trauma, distress).
